persons.name: Vijay Singh Yadav
persons.context: Vijay Singh Yadav was the deceased in this case, allegedly murdered in Delhi. The prosecution claimed he was killed as part of a contract killing motivated by financial gain.

persons.name: Hitender @ Chhotu
persons.context: Hitender @ Chhotu was identified as the main accused and alleged gang leader in the murder conspiracy. He was charged under Section 302 (Murder) and Section 120B (Criminal conspiracy) of the IPC.

persons.name: Deepak @ Charu
persons.context: Deepak @ Charu was named as one of the accused in the case, involved in the alleged conspiracy to murder Vijay Singh Yadav. He was charged under relevant sections of the IPC for murder and criminal conspiracy.

persons.name: Bhisham
persons.context: Bhisham was listed among the accused persons in the criminal conspiracy to commit murder. He was charged under Section 302 and Section 120B of the IPC.

persons.name: Desh Raj
persons.context: Desh Raj was named as an accused in the case, alleged to be part of the conspiracy to murder Vijay Singh Yadav. He faced charges under the IPC for murder and criminal conspiracy.

persons.name: Rishi Pal
persons.context: Rishi Pal was one of the accused in the case, implicated in the criminal conspiracy to kill the victim. He was charged under Section 302 and Section 120B of the IPC.

persons.name: Ashok Jain
persons.context: Ashok Jain was named as an accused in the murder conspiracy. He was charged under the IPC for involvement in the alleged contract killing.

persons.name: Gopal Krishan Aggarwal
persons.context: Gopal Krishan Aggarwal was listed among the accused in the case, alleged to have participated in the conspiracy to murder Vijay Singh Yadav. He faced charges under the IPC for murder and criminal conspiracy.

title: State vs cg
caseText:
1. Case Identity
Title: State vs. Hitender @ Chhotu & Others
Court: Additional Sessions Judge‑01, Tis Hazari Courts, Delhi
FIR Involved: FIR No. 356/2007 (Hauz Qazi Police Station)
Judgment Date: 21 April 2012
Public Judgment Source: Indian Kanoon public record �
Indian Kanoon
2. Incident Summary
A murder was alleged: deceased Vijay Singh Yadav was killed in Delhi.
The prosecution’s claim was that several accused hired a criminal gang to murder him for pecuniary gain (contract killing) through shared criminal motives.
Multiple accused were charged, including Hitender @ Chhotu, Ashok Jain, Rishi Pal, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and others — alleged to be part of a conspiracy to murder. �
Indian Kanoon
3. Accused Persons
Main accused named in the case judgment include:
Hitender @ Chhotu (alleged gang leader)
Deepak @ Charu
Bhisham
Desh Raj
Rishi Pal
Ashok Jain
Gopal Krishan Aggarwal
(For your simulator you can focus on a subset of these.) �
Indian Kanoon
4. Legal Charges
Main Criminal Offences
Section 302 of IPC – Murder
Section 120B of IPC – Criminal conspiracy
(Prosecution also attempted charges under the MCOCA organized crime act, which became central in dispute.)

transcript.speaker: Court
transcript.content: Court is now in session. The Honorable Judge presiding. This is the matter of: State vs cg. All parties are present. Proceedings will begin with Opening Statements.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: We are innocent your honour

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
Order! The court acknowledges the presence of all parties. This is the matter of *State versus Hitender @ Chhotu & Others*, arising from FIR No. 356/2007 registered at Hauz Qazi Police Station, Delhi. The accused stand charged under Section 302 read with Section 120B of the Indian Penal Code for the alleged murder of Vijay Singh Yadav, with the prosecution further invoking the provisions of the Maharashtra Control of Organised Crime Act (MCOCA), though that aspect remains subject to legal determination.

At this stage—the Prosecution Case—the burden rests squarely upon the State to prove the charges beyond reasonable doubt. The court expects the prosecution to present its evidence methodically: through witnesses, documentary exhibits, and any forensic or circumstantial material that supports the allegations of conspiracy and murder.

Counsel for the prosecution, you may proceed with your opening statement. The court is ready to hear your case.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor, may it please the Court,

The State stands before you today to present a clear, cogent, and compelling case of cold-blooded murder born out of greed, orchestrated through a criminal conspiracy, and executed with ruthless precision. The deceased, Vijay Singh Yadav, was not a random victim—he was a target. A target selected, paid for, and eliminated because his life stood in the way of financial gain for the accused.

The evidence will show that this was not a crime of passion or a momentary lapse in judgment. This was a premeditated, contract-style killing—planned, funded, and carried out by individuals who treated human life as a commodity. The prosecution will prove beyond any reasonable doubt that the accused—Hitender @ Chhotu, Ashok Jain, Rishi Pal, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and their co-conspirators—entered into a criminal conspiracy under Section 120B of the Indian Penal Code to commit the murder of Vijay Singh Yadav, and that they successfully executed that plan on the date in question, thereby committing the offense of murder under Section 302 IPC.

The motive was pecuniary. The method was organized. The coordination was deliberate. Phone records, witness testimonies, financial transactions, and forensic evidence will converge to paint an incontrovertible picture: these accused did not act alone—they acted as part of a coordinated criminal enterprise.

We will present eyewitnesses who saw the assailants at the scene. We will introduce call detail records showing frequent and suspicious communications between the accused in the days leading up to the murder—communications that ceased abruptly after the crime. We will show that payments were made, instructions were given, and roles were assigned. The accused Hitender @ Chhotu, known as a gang leader, was not merely present—he was the operational brain behind the execution. Others, including Ashok Jain and Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, provided the financial backing and logistical support. Rishi Pal and the remaining accused facilitated the movement, identification, and elimination of the target.

The crime was not spontaneous. It was the culmination of a carefully constructed plan—hatched in secrecy, funded through illicit means, and carried out with the precision of a professional hit. The State will demonstrate that every element of criminal conspiracy and murder has been satisfied. The chain of evidence is unbroken. The intent is clear. The act is proven.

We do not ask this Court to convict based on suspicion or innuendo. We ask you to convict based on evidence—evidence that will show, beyond any shadow of doubt, that the accused are guilty of the charges framed against them.

The State is ready to present its case. We call our first witness.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content: Ok

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court acknowledges the prosecution’s opening statement. The State has outlined its theory of the case—namely, that the accused conspired for pecuniary gain to orchestrate the murder of Vijay Singh Yadav in a premeditated, contract-style killing, with coordination among multiple individuals including Hitender @ Chhotu, Ashok Jain, Rishi Pal, and Gopal Krishan Aggarwal.

At this stage, the burden remains upon the prosecution to substantiate these grave allegations with credible, admissible evidence. Mere assertions, however compelling in tone, are not proof. The court will require concrete testimony, reliable documentation, forensic corroboration, and legally sound linkages to establish both the actus reus and the mens rea of the offenses charged under Sections 302 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code.

The invocation of MCOCA, while noted, remains subject to strict scrutiny—particularly regarding whether the alleged acts meet the statutory threshold of “organized crime” as defined under that enactment. However, that determination may be deferred until after the full evidentiary record is laid.

Prosecution, you may now call your first witness. Ensure that all exhibits are properly marked and that foundational requirements for admissibility are satisfied. The court expects orderly and efficient presentation of evidence. Proceed.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor, the State calls its first witness: Inspector Rakesh Kumar, the investigating officer in this case.

[WITNESS IS SWORN IN]

Your Honor, may I proceed with the direct examination?

Inspector Kumar, please state your full name, designation, and the capacity in which you are appearing before this Court today.

[WITNESS]: I am Inspector Rakesh Kumar, attached to Hauz Qazi Police Station, Delhi. I was the investigating officer assigned to FIR No. 356/2007, registered on 14th August 2007, concerning the murder of Vijay Singh Yadav.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: Inspector Kumar, could you briefly outline the sequence of events that led to the registration of this FIR and the initiation of the investigation?

[WITNESS]: On the morning of 14th August 2007, at approximately 6:15 a.m., we received information through wireless that a man had been found dead near the rear entrance of the municipal school in the Hauz Qazi area. Upon reaching the spot, we found the body of a male, later identified as Vijay Singh Yadav, aged about 38 years, bearing multiple stab wounds to the chest and abdomen. The body was still warm, and a blood trail suggested he had attempted to flee before collapsing. No weapon was found at the scene.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: And what steps did you take immediately thereafter?

[WITNESS]: We secured the scene, called the forensic team, and sent the body for post-mortem. Simultaneously, we began canvassing the area. Two local shopkeepers—Shri Ram Prasad and Smt. Usha Devi—came forward and stated they had seen two men on a motorcycle, wearing dark clothing and helmets, fleeing the area around 5:50 a.m. One of them was seen wiping what appeared to be a blood-stained object with a cloth.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: Did the post-mortem report corroborate the cause of death?

[WITNESS]: Yes, Your Honor. The post-mortem conducted by Dr. Anil Mehra at LNJP Hospital concluded that the cause of death was hemorrhagic shock due to multiple stab injuries to the thoracic and abdominal regions. The nature of the wounds—deep, precise, and clustered—indicated a deliberate and forceful attack, consistent with an intent to kill.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: Inspector, turning to the investigation—what led you to suspect a conspiracy behind this murder?

[WITNESS]: Initially, it appeared to be a random robbery. But during our inquiry, we discovered that the deceased, Vijay Singh Yadav, was a witness in a property dispute case involving Ashok Jain and Gopal Krishan Aggarwal. Both had filed civil suits against him over a disputed plot in Old Delhi. Moreover, bank records showed that in the three weeks prior to the murder, Aggarwal had withdrawn ₹1.2 lakh in cash, and Jain had transferred ₹75,000 to an untraceable account. These financial movements coincided with increased call activity between Jain, Aggarwal, and one Hitender @ Chhotu—a known associate with prior involvement in extortion and hired violence.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: Did you obtain call detail records (CDRs) during your investigation?

[WITNESS]: Yes, Your Honor. We secured CDRs for the mobile numbers linked to Ashok Jain, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and Hitender @ Chhotu. The records show frequent calls between these numbers in the 10 days leading up to the murder—sometimes multiple calls per day. Notably, on 13th August, the day before the killing, there were 14 calls between Jain and Chhotu, and 9 between Aggarwal and Chhotu. After 14th August, all communication between them ceased abruptly.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: And what about the accused Hitender @ Chhotu? How was he connected to the actual commission of the crime?

[WITNESS]: During custodial interrogation of a co-accused, Deepak @ Charu—who was arrested in a separate case—we obtained a disclosure statement indicating that Hitender @ Chhotu had recruited him and one Bhisham to carry out the killing. Deepak stated that Chhotu had shown him a photograph of the deceased, provided ₹50,000 as advance payment, and instructed them to “finish the job” near the school, where Vijay Singh Yadav was known to take his morning walk. The motorcycle used in the escape—a black Pulsar—was later recovered from a scrap dealer in Chandni Chowk, who identified Bhisham as the person who sold it to him on 15th August.

[PROSECUTION COUNSEL]: Inspector, were any recoveries made pursuant to disclosure

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has heard the testimony of Inspector Rakesh Kumar, the investigating officer in FIR No. 356/2007. His account outlines a sequence of investigative steps, including the discovery of the body, the post-mortem findings, eyewitness accounts of fleeing assailants on a motorcycle, financial transactions involving the accused, and call detail records showing heightened communication in the days preceding the murder.

While the prosecution has laid a foundation suggesting a possible conspiracy—citing motive, financial movements, and suspicious telephonic activity—the court observes that much of the evidence presented thus far remains circumstantial. The testimony regarding the disclosure statement of Deepak @ Charu is particularly significant, but such statements, especially when obtained during custodial interrogation, require careful scrutiny and corroboration under Section 27 of the Indian Evidence Act. The recovery of the motorcycle, if properly linked to the accused, may assist in establishing a connection, but chain of custody and identification must be firmly established.

Furthermore, while the call detail records indicate frequent communication between Ashok Jain, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and Hitender @ Chhotu prior to the incident, the prosecution must demonstrate, through admissible evidence, that these communications pertained to the commission of the murder and not to any legitimate or unrelated matter. Mere proximity in time and frequency does not, by itself, prove criminal conspiracy under Section 120B IPC.

The court notes that the prosecution has invoked the Maharashtra Control of Organised Crime Act (MCOCA), but no foundational evidence has yet been presented to satisfy the stringent threshold under Section 2(1)(d) of MCOCA, which requires proof of a “continuing unlawful activity” by an “organised crime syndicate.” At this stage, the applicability of MCOCA remains legally and factually unsubstantiated.

Prosecution, you may continue with your examination-in-chief of Inspector Kumar, but be advised: the court will require clear, credible, and legally admissible evidence—not inferences or assumptions—to sustain the charges under Sections 302 and 120B IPC. Ensure that all documentary evidence, including CDRs, bank records, and recovery memos, are properly exhibited and authenticated in accordance with the Indian Evidence Act.

You may proceed.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor,

The State continues to build its case with methodical precision, and we now present the next critical piece of evidence—one that directly links the accused to the commission of this heinous crime.

With the Court’s permission, we now seek to formally exhibit the Call Detail Records (CDRs) for the mobile numbers used by Ashok Jain, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and Hitender @ Chhotu during the period of 1st August to 15th August 2007. These records, obtained through lawful process from the concerned telecom service providers and duly authenticated under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, reveal a pattern of communication that is not merely suspicious—it is incriminating.

The CDRs show that in the ten days leading up to the murder, there were over 87 calls exchanged between these three accused. Notably, on 12th August, the day Ashok Jain withdrew ₹75,000 in cash from his account at Punjab National Bank, Chandni Chowk branch—a fact we will corroborate with bank statements—he made six consecutive calls to Hitender @ Chhotu. Similarly, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, who withdrew ₹1.2 lakh in a single transaction on 10th August, initiated 11 calls to Chhotu between 10th and 13th August. The frequency, timing, and abrupt cessation of all communication after 14th August cannot be dismissed as coincidence. This is the hallmark of coordination—of planning, funding, and execution.

We further submit the certified copy of the bank withdrawal slips from Punjab National Bank and State Bank of India, marked as Exhibits P-1 and P-2, which confirm these large cash withdrawals in the immediate lead-up to the murder. The prosecution contends that these funds were used to finance the contract killing—₹50,000 paid in advance to Deepak @ Charu, as disclosed during custodial interrogation and corroborated by the recovery of the blood-stained cloth and the motorcycle sold within 24 hours of the murder.

Moreover, Your Honor, we now call upon Smt. Usha Devi, a shopkeeper who operates a tea stall near the municipal school. She will testify that at approximately 5:45 a.m. on 14th August, she saw two men on a black Pulsar motorcycle—one riding, the other holding a long, cloth-wrapped object. She distinctly recalls the rider removing his helmet momentarily to wipe his face, and she identified Hitender @ Chhotu in a police identification parade conducted on 20th August 2007—a procedure video-recorded and available for the Court’s review.

This identification, coupled with the CDRs, the financial transactions, and the recovery of the motorcycle from a scrap dealer who identified Bhisham as the seller, forms an unbroken chain of circumstantial evidence pointing directly to the guilt of the accused.

The defense may argue that these are mere inferences—but the law does not require the State to produce a signed confession or an eyewitness to the stabbing. What is required is proof beyond reasonable doubt, and we submit that the convergence of motive, means, opportunity, and coordination—supported by documentary, forensic, and testimonial evidence—more than satisfies that burden.

We are not asking the Court to convict on suspicion. We are asking you to convict on the cumulative weight of evidence that leaves no room for reasonable doubt.

The State rests its case-in-chief at this stage and submits that a prima facie case has been established against all accused under Sections 302 and 120B IPC. We reserve the right to present rebuttal evidence after the defense presents its case.

Thank you, Your Honor.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor,

The defense rises not to dispute the tragedy of Vijay Singh Yadav’s death—no one in this courtroom denies that a life was lost—but to assert, with firm conviction, that the State has failed to prove, beyond reasonable doubt, that my clients—Hitender @ Chhotu, Ashok Jain, Rishi Pal, or Gopal Krishan Aggarwal—had any role in that death, let alone that they conspired to commit murder.

What the prosecution has presented today is not evidence—it is speculation wrapped in circumstantial coincidence. They speak of phone calls, cash withdrawals, and a recovered motorcycle as if these facts, standing alone, constitute proof of guilt. But under our law, suspicion is not substitute for proof. And correlation is not causation.

Let us begin with the so-called “disclosure statement” of Deepak @ Charu. This statement, extracted during custodial interrogation—a setting notorious for coercion and fabrication—is inadmissible as substantive evidence under Section 25 of the Indian Evidence Act. Even if partially admitted under Section 27 for recovery, it cannot establish the truth of the confession itself. The prosecution relies on it to claim that Hitender @ Chhotu recruited killers and paid them ₹50,000. But where is the corroboration? Where is the forensic link between that money and my client? Where is the independent witness to this alleged transaction? There is none. Only the word of an accused in police custody—a man with every incentive to shift blame onto others to save his own skin.

Now, consider the call detail records. Yes, there were calls between Ashok Jain, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and Hitender @ Chhotu in the days before the murder. But the prosecution offers no context—no content, no intercepted conversations, no proof that these calls were about murder. These men are businessmen. They have disputes. They communicate. That is not a crime. To infer conspiracy from frequency alone is to criminalize normal human interaction. And the abrupt end to calls after 14th August? Perhaps they resolved their civil matter. Perhaps they simply stopped talking. But the State asks this Court to assume the worst—without a shred of direct evidence.

As for the financial transactions: cash withdrawals are not crimes. People withdraw money for countless legitimate reasons—medical emergencies, property deals, family expenses. The prosecution points to ₹1.2 lakh and ₹75,000 as suspicious, but provides no nexus between those sums and the alleged murder payment. No ledger, no receipt, no forensic tracing of the currency. The ₹50,000 allegedly paid to Deepak? Never recovered. Never accounted for. Mere assertion.

And the eyewitness, Smt. Usha Devi? She claims to have identified Hitender @ Chhotu in a police identification parade. But identification parades conducted weeks after the incident, under police supervision, are inherently unreliable—especially when the witness saw the accused for mere seconds in dim morning light, wearing a helmet. The Supreme Court has repeatedly cautioned against overreliance on such identifications, particularly when tainted by prior exposure or suggestive procedures. Was this parade truly fair? Was it video-recorded in its entirety, or only selectively? The defense reserves the right to challenge its admissibility and credibility during cross-examination.

Finally, Your Honor, the motorcycle. Recovered from a scrap dealer who claims Bhisham sold it. But Bhisham is not here to testify. The scrap dealer’s statement is hearsay unless he is produced in court. And even if believed, selling a motorcycle after a crime does not prove involvement in the crime—only that someone sold a bike. The chain of custody is broken. The link to my client is nonexistent.

The prosecution asks this Court to convict based on a web of assumptions: that because these men had disputes with the deceased, they must have killed him; that because they made calls, they must have plotted murder; that because cash was withdrawn, it must have been for a hit. But that is not proof. That is prejudice.

Under Section 101 of the Indian Evidence Act, the burden is on the State to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. That burden has not been met. The evidence is fragmented, uncorroborated, and open to multiple innocent explanations. Reasonable doubt exists—not as a technicality, but as a fundamental principle of justice.

We do not deny that a murder occurred. But we do deny that the prosecution has identified the guilty parties correctly. To convict my clients on this record would be to substitute conjecture for justice.

The defense will demonstrate, through cross-examination and our own evidence, that the investigation was rushed, the evidence selectively interpreted, and the accused scapegoated to close a case. We ask this Court to see through the smoke and recognize the absence of fire.

Thank you, Your Honor.

transcript.speaker: The Honorable Judge
transcript.content:
The court has carefully considered the arguments and evidence presented during the prosecution’s case-in-chief.

While the State has laid out a narrative suggesting a possible conspiracy to murder Vijay Singh Yadav—citing motive rooted in a property dispute, suspicious financial transactions, frequent telephonic communications among the accused, and certain recoveries—the court must emphasize that the threshold for conviction, particularly under Sections 302 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code, remains proof beyond reasonable doubt.

At this juncture, the evidence remains largely circumstantial. The call detail records, while indicating increased communication between Ashok Jain, Gopal Krishan Aggarwal, and Hitender @ Chhotu in the days preceding the murder, do not, in themselves, establish the content or criminal intent of those conversations. Absent intercepted communications, voice analysis, or corroborative testimony linking these calls directly to the planning or execution of the murder, their probative value remains limited.

Similarly, cash withdrawals—though notable—are not inherently incriminating. The prosecution has failed to produce any tangible link between the withdrawn amounts and the alleged payment for the killing. The claimed advance of ₹50,000 to Deepak @ Charu rests solely on a custodial disclosure statement, which, under Section 25 of the Indian Evidence Act, is inadmissible as proof of the fact stated. Any recovery purportedly based on such a statement must be independently corroborated, and the chain of custody—particularly regarding the motorcycle and cloth—must be rigorously established.

The eyewitness identification by Smt. Usha Devi raises further concerns. Identification parades conducted weeks after the incident, especially under police supervision and involving a witness who observed the accused for only a fleeting moment in low light and while partially obscured by a helmet, carry inherent risks of suggestibility and error. The Supreme Court has repeatedly cautioned against relying solely on such identifications without strong corroboration.

Furthermore, the applicability of the Maharashtra Control of Organised Crime Act (MCOCA) has not been substantiated. No evidence has been presented to demonstrate that the accused were part of an “organised crime syndicate” engaged in a “continuing unlawful activity,” as required under Section 2(1)(d) of MCOCA. Invoking MCOCA requires more than mere allegations of hired violence; it demands proof of a structured, ongoing criminal enterprise—proof that is conspicuously absent at this stage.

That said, the court acknowledges that a prima facie case may exist warranting the framing of charges. The convergence of motive, timing, financial movements, and suspicious communications—while insufficient for conviction—cannot be dismissed outright. The defense will have full opportunity to challenge this evidence through cross-examination and to present its own case.

Accordingly, the court directs that charges under Sections 302 and 120B IPC shall be framed against the accused. The question of MCOCA applicability shall be revisited after the conclusion of all evidence.

The prosecution has presented its case-in-chief. The defense may now proceed with cross-examination of Inspector Rakesh Kumar, or, if it so chooses, may reserve cross-examination until after the formal framing of charges. The court expects both sides to adhere strictly to procedural fairness and the rules of evidence.

We will now adjourn briefly to allow for the formal drafting and reading of charges. The court will reconvene shortly for that purpose.

transcript.speaker: Prosecution Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor,

The State respectfully submits that the evidence presented—though necessarily circumstantial in parts—forms a coherent, compelling, and unbroken chain that establishes, beyond reasonable doubt, the guilt of the accused under Sections 302 and 120B of the Indian Penal Code.

Let us begin with motive. The deceased, Vijay Singh Yadav, was not a random victim. He was a key witness in a high-stakes property dispute involving two of the accused—Ashok Jain and Gopal Krishan Aggarwal. Civil litigation over valuable land in Old Delhi had reached a critical juncture, and the deceased’s testimony posed a direct threat to their claims. This is not mere speculation; it is documented in court records and confirmed by the investigating officer. When financial interest collides with legal vulnerability, the temptation to eliminate the obstacle becomes overwhelming—and the evidence shows that temptation was acted upon.

Now, consider the means. Large cash withdrawals—₹1.2 lakh by Aggarwal, ₹75,000 by Jain—within days of each other, at a time when no legitimate business explanation has been offered. These are not routine transactions. They are sudden, substantial, and unexplained. The prosecution does not suggest that withdrawing cash is a crime—but when such withdrawals coincide precisely with the planning and execution of a murder, they cease to be innocent and become instrumental. The ₹50,000 allegedly paid to Deepak @ Charu as advance—though not recovered—is corroborated by the recovery of the motorcycle used in the crime, sold within 24 hours of the murder. That motorcycle was traced, identified, and linked to Bhisham, who acted under the direction of Hitender @ Chhotu. This is not coincidence; this is logistics.

Then there is the communication pattern. Over 87 calls in ten days between the three principal accused—peaking on the very days of the cash withdrawals and culminating in 23 calls on 13th August alone. The prosecution does not need to play recordings or reveal content to prove conspiracy. The law recognizes that conspirators often speak in codes, in hints, in silences. What matters is the timing, the frequency, and the abrupt cessation—because once the deed was done, there was no need to talk anymore. This pattern is not explained by civil disputes or business dealings. It is explained by coordination.

And let us not forget the eyewitness. Smt. Usha Devi, a local shopkeeper with no motive to lie, saw two men on a black Pulsar at 5:45 a.m.—fleeing the scene, one clutching a cloth-wrapped object. She identified Hitender @ Chhotu in a duly conducted identification parade, video-recorded and compliant with procedural safeguards. The defense calls this unreliable—but the law does not require perfect visibility or prolonged observation. It requires credible identification, and hers was clear, consistent, and unshaken.

As for the disclosure statement of Deepak @ Charu—yes, it is not admissible as proof of the confession itself. But under Section 27 of the Indian Evidence Act, the information leading to the discovery of the motorcycle is admissible. And that motorcycle—recovered, documented, and traced—is real. It ties the accused to the escape, to the crime, to the cover-up.

Your Honor, the defense asks this Court to treat each piece of evidence in isolation and find it wanting. But the law demands we view the totality of the circumstances. When motive, means, opportunity, financial movement, communication patterns, eyewitness testimony, and recoveries all converge—when they point in one direction, with no plausible alternative explanation—then reasonable doubt vanishes.

This was not a crime of passion. It was a contract killing, coldly planned, funded, and executed. The accused did not act alone—they acted in concert, with shared intent, with shared purpose. That is criminal conspiracy. That is murder.

The State has met its burden. We ask this Court to recognize the strength of the evidence, to uphold the charges under Sections 302 and 120B IPC, and to ensure that justice is not denied because the killers wore helmets or spoke in whispers.

Thank you, Your Honor.

transcript.speaker: Defense Counsel
transcript.content:
Your Honor,

The prosecution asks this Court to convict my clients based on a web of speculation—on coincidence dressed as conspiracy, on suspicion masquerading as proof. But the law demands more. It demands certainty. It demands evidence that eliminates reasonable doubt—not evidence that merely points a finger.

Let us begin with the foundation of the prosecution’s case: motive. Yes, Ashok Jain and Gopal Krishan Aggarwal were involved in a civil property dispute with the deceased. But a civil dispute is not a death sentence. It is not proof of murder. Millions of Indians litigate over property every year without resorting to assassination. To suggest that because these men had a legal disagreement, they must have hired killers is to criminalize disagreement itself. Where is the evidence of threats? Of violent history? Of any prior connection to organized crime or contract killing? There is none. Only assumption.

Now, the so-called “financial trail.” Cash withdrawals. The prosecution treats ₹1.2 lakh and ₹75,000 as damning sums—but offers no context. Were these funds used for the alleged killing? No currency was recovered. No serial numbers traced. No witness saw the money change hands. The ₹50,000 allegedly paid to Deepak @ Charu exists only in a custodial disclosure—a statement extracted in police custody, where coercion is not the exception but the norm. Under Section 25 of the Indian Evidence Act, such a statement is inadmissible as proof of the fact confessed. Even under Section 27, only the recovery of the motorcycle is admissible—not the narrative of murder for hire. And yet, the prosecution builds its entire case on that narrative.

And what of the motorcycle? Recovered from a scrap dealer who claims Bhisham sold it. But Bhisham is not here to testify. The scrap dealer’s statement is hearsay unless he is produced in open court. Even if believed, selling a motorcycle after a crime does not prove involvement in the crime. It proves only that a bike was sold. The chain of custody is broken. The link to my client Hitender @ Chhotu is nonexistent.

The call detail records—frequent calls, yes. But contentless. Contextless. These men are businessmen with disputes. They communicate. That is not a crime. The prosecution asks you to infer conspiracy from silence—from the absence of calls after the murder. But perhaps they resolved their matter. Perhaps they simply stopped talking. To assume criminal intent from communication patterns alone is to abandon reason for suspicion.

And the eyewitness, Smt. Usha Devi? She saw two men on a motorcycle in the dim light of early morning, one wearing a helmet. She claims to have identified Hitender @ Chhotu weeks later in a police-conducted parade. But identification under such conditions—fleeting glance, partial concealment, delayed procedure—is inherently unreliable. The Supreme Court has repeatedly warned against such identifications, especially when conducted under police supervision and without strict compliance with procedural safeguards. Was the parade truly fair? Was it video-recorded in full, or selectively? The burden is on the State to prove its fairness—not on the accused to prove its taint.

Your Honor, the prosecution speaks of an “unbroken chain” of evidence. But a chain is only as strong as its weakest link—and here, every link is fractured. There is no forensic link between my clients and the crime scene. No weapon. No bloodstains. No fingerprints. No DNA. The body was found, the crime occurred—but the connection to my clients rests entirely on inference, on coincidence, on the word of an accused in custody.

The burden is on the State to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. That burden has not been met. Reasonable doubt exists—not as a technicality, but as a fundamental principle of justice. To convict my clients on this record would be to substitute conjecture for evidence, and fear for fairness.

We do not deny that a murder occurred. But we do deny that the prosecution has proven who committed it. The real killers may still be at large. The real conspiracy may remain uncovered. But to punish the wrong men to close a case is not justice—it is its opposite.

I ask this Court to see through the smoke of suspicion and recognize the absence of fire. The charges must fail.

Thank you, Your Honor.
